translations.reference: Traditional, Complementary and Integrative Medicine Overview: Traditional medicine Traditional medicine has a long history. It is the sum total of the knowledge, skill, and practices based on the theories, beliefs, and experiences indigenous to different cultures, whether explicable or not, used in the maintenance of health as well as in the prevention, diagnosis, improvement or treatment of physical and mental illness. Complementary medicine The terms “complementary medicine” or “alternative medicine” refer to a broad set of health care practices that are not part of that country’s own tradition or conventional medicine and are not fully integrated into the dominant health-care system. They are used interchangeably with traditional medicine in some countries. Herbal medicines Herbal medicines include herbs, herbal materials, herbal preparations and finished herbal products, that contain as active ingredients parts of plants, or other plant materials, or combinations. Impact: The WHO Traditional Medicine Strategy 2014–2023 was developed and launched in response to the World Health Assembly resolution on traditional medicine (WHA62.13).
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the provided Zulu text into English:

**Traditional Medicine, Complementary and Alternative Medicine: A General Overview**

Traditional medicine has a long history. It is a comprehensive system of knowledge, skills, and practices based on the beliefs, values, and traditional knowledge of various cultures, whether tangible or intangible, used in the care of health and in preventing, diagnosing, treating, or curing physical and mental illness.

**Complementary Medicine**

The terms “complementary medicine” or “alternative medicine” refer to a broad collection of healthcare practices that are not part of the traditional culture of a country or conventional medicine and are not fully integrated into the dominant healthcare system. It is used interchangeably with traditional medicine in some countries.

**Herbal Medicine**

Herbal medicine includes various remedies, herbal products, herbal preparations, and finished herbal products, containing active ingredients from plant parts, or other plant materials, or combinations thereof.

**Impact:**

The WHO’s Traditional Medicine Strategy 2014–2023 was developed and launched in response to the World Health Assembly resolution on traditional medicine (WHA62.13).

translations.source: Isu lihlose ukusekela Amazwe Angamalungu ekuthuthukiseni izinqubomgomo ezisebenzayo kanye nokusebenzisa izinhlelo zokusebenza ezizoqinisa indima edlalwa imithi yendabuko ekugcineni imiphakathi iphilile. Ukubhekana nezinselele, ukusabela ezidingweni ezihlonzwe Amazwe Angamalungu kanye nokwakhela phezu komsebenzi owenziwe ngaphansi kwesu lemithi yendabuko yeWHO: 2002-2005, isu elibuyekeziwe lesikhathi sika-2014-2023 linikezela ukunaka okungaphezulu kunokwandulela walo ekubekeni phambili izinsiza zezempilo nezinhlelo. , okuhlanganisa imikhiqizo yemithi yesintu nehambisanayo, izinqubo kanye nodokotela. Izinjongo zamasu yilezi: Ukwakha isisekelo solwazi sokuphatha okusebenzayo kweT&CM ngokusebenzisa izinqubomgomo zikazwelonke ezifanele; Ukuqinisa ukuqinisekiswa kwekhwalithi, ukuphepha, ukusetshenziswa okufanele nokusebenza kahle kweT&CM ngokulawula imikhiqizo, izinqubo kanye nabasebenzi; Ukukhuthaza ukuhlinzekwa kwezempilo emhlabeni wonke ngokuhlanganisa izinsiza zeT&CM ekulethweni kwezinsizakalo zokunakekelwa kwezempilo kanye nokuzinakekela. Impendulo yeWHO: Umbiko wembulunga yonke weWHO ngemithi yendabuko nehambisanayo 2019 wathuthukiswa ukuze kubhekwane negebe kudatha ethembekile, ethembekile nesemthethweni evela emazweni Angamalungu endaweni yeT&CM. Lo mbiko ubuyekeza inqubekelaphambili yomhlaba kuT&CM kule minyaka engamashumi amabili edlule futhi usekelwe emnikelweni ovela emazweni Angamalungu eWHO ayi-179. Ihlinzeka ngolwazi olubalulekile kubenzi bezinqubomgomo, ochwepheshe bezempilo kanye nomphakathi ukuze usebenzise imali engaba khona yeT&CM empilweni nasenhlalakahleni.
translations.reference: The strategy aims to support Member States in developing proactive policies and implementing action plans that will strengthen the role traditional medicine plays in keeping populations healthy. Addressing the challenges, responding to the needs identified by Member States and building on the work done under the WHO traditional medicine strategy: 2002–2005, the updated strategy for the period 2014–2023 devotes more attention than its predecessor to prioritizing health services and systems, including traditional and complementary medicine products, practices and practitioners. The strategic objectives are: To build the knowledge base for active management of T&CM through appropriate national policies; To strengthen the quality assurance, safety, proper use and effectiveness of T&CM by regulating products, practices and practitioners; To promote universal health coverage by integrating T&CM services into health care service delivery and self-health care. WHO response: The WHO global report on traditional and complementary medicine 2019 was developed to address the gap in reliable, credible and official data from Member States in the area of T&CM. This report reviews global progress in T&CM over the past two decades and is based on contributions from 179 WHO Member States. It provides valuable information for policy-makers, health professionals and the public for capitalizing on the potential contribution of T&CM to health and well-being.

"The strategy aims to support Member States in developing effective policies and utilizing programs that will strengthen the role of traditional medicine in maintaining healthy communities. To address challenges, respond to the needs identified by Member States, and build upon the work done under the WHO strategy on traditional medicine 2002-2005, the revised strategy for 2014-2023 provides greater focus on expanding its scope to prioritize traditional medicine and health systems resources, including traditional medicine products and practices, procedures, and practitioners. The strategy's objectives are these: To build a knowledge base for the effective management of T&CM through appropriate national policies; To strengthen the assurance of the quality, safety, proper use, and effectiveness of T&CM by regulating products, procedures, and practitioners; To promote global health by integrating T&CM resources into the delivery of health services and self-care. WHO Response: The WHO’s comprehensive report on traditional and complementary medicine 2019 was developed to address the gap in reliable, credible, and official data from Member States in the area of T&CM. This report reviews global progress on T&CM over the past two decades and is based on contributions from 179 WHO Member States. It provides valuable information to policymakers, health professionals, and the public to utilize available resources for T&CM in health and well-being."

**Key Notes on the Translation:**

*   **T&CM:** I've consistently used "T&CM" (Traditional and Complementary Medicine) as it's the standard abbreviation in this context.
*   **Accuracy:** I've aimed for a translation that is both accurate and reads naturally in English.
*   **Context:** The document appears to be related to a WHO strategy, so I've maintained the formal tone appropriate for that context.

translations.source: Ukufakelwa kabusha Uhlolojikelele: Ukufakelwa kabusha kwamangqamuzana omuntu, izicubu nezitho zomzimba Ukufakelwa kabusha kwamangqamuzana omuntu, izicubu noma izitho kusindisa izimpilo eziningi futhi kubuyisela imisebenzi ebalulekile lapho kungekho ezinye izindlela ezisebenzayo ezikhona. Eminyakeni engu-50, ukufakelwa kabusha sekuyinto ephumelelayo emhlabeni wonke. Kodwa ke, kunomehluko omkhulu phakathi kwamazwe ekufinyeleleni ukufakelwa okulungile kanye nezinga lokuphepha, ikhwalithi, ukusebenza kahle kokunikela kanye nokufakelwa kwamangqamuzana omuntu, izicubu nezitho zomzimba. Izici zokuziphatha zokufakelwa kabusha zihamba phambili. Ikakhulukazi, izidingo zeziguli ezingahlangatshezwana nazo kanye nokushoda kokufakelwa kabusha kuholela esilingweni sokushushumbiswa kwezingxenye zomzimba womuntu ukuze zifakwe kabusha. Ukufakelwa komuntu Amaseli omuntu kanye nezicubu zokufakelwa kabusha kungasindisa izimpilo noma kubuyisele imisebenzi ebalulekile. Isibonelo, icorneal graft ingabuyisela ukubona ebumpumputheni be-cornea;
translations.reference: Transplantation Overview: Transplantation of human cells, tissues and organs Transplantation of human cells, tissues or organs saves many lives and restores essential functions where no alternatives of comparable effectiveness exist. In 50 years, transplantation has become a successful worldwide practice. However, there are large differences between countries in access to suitable transplantation and in the level of safety, quality, efficacy of donation and transplantation of human cells, tissues and organs. The ethical aspects of transplantation are at the forefront. In particular, the unmet patients’ needs and the shortage of transplants lead to the temptation of trafficking in human body components for transplantation. Human transplantation Human cells and tissues for transplantation can save lives or restore essential functions. For examplea corneal graft can restore sight in corneal blindness;
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Zulu document into English:

**Re-transplantation Overview: Re-transplantation of human organs, tissues, and body parts**

Re-transplantation of human organs, tissues, or parts saves many lives and restores vital functions when no other viable options exist. For 50 years, re-transplantation has been a successful practice worldwide. However, there is a significant difference between countries in terms of access to proper re-transplantation and the level of safety, quality, efficacy of donation, and re-transplantation of human organs, tissues, and body parts. Ethical aspects of re-transplantation are evolving. Specifically, the unmet needs of patients and the shortage of re-transplantation have led to the illegal trafficking of human body parts for re-transplantation. **Human Cell and Tissue Re-transplantation** can save lives or restore vital functions. For example, a corneal graft can restore sight in patients with corneal damage.

translations.source: ukufakelwa kabusha kwehaematopoietic stem cell kungalapha izifo zokuzalwa noma ezitholiwe kubandakanya neleukemia Bona ulwazi olwengeziwe ngamaseli weHaematopoeitic Stem Cells ukufakelwa kwe-valve yenhliziyo yomuntu kuvame ukwenza isimo esingcono kakhulu sokubuyisela futhi iziguli ezithola usizo azidingi ukwelashwa kwesikhathi eside okulwa nokuhlangana. I-Xenotransplantation IXenotransplantation, isilwane ukuya kumuntu, okuchazwa njengamaseli aphilayo, izicubu noma izitho zemvelaphi yezilwane kanye noketshezi lomzimba womuntu, amaseli, izicubu noma izitho ezine-ex vivo nalezi zinto eziphilayo, zexenogeneic, zinamandla okwenza enye into yemvelaphi yomuntu kanye nokuvala ukushoda kwezinto ezisetshenziswa abantu zokufakelwa kabusha. Umnikelo: Iminikelo evela kubantu abashonile kufanele ithuthukiswe ukuze ibe namandla abo aphezulu okwelapha ukugwema, noma nini lapho kunokwenzeka, izingozi ezingokwemvelo zokunikela abaphilayo, njengoba kushiwo Kumgomo 3 Wokuqondisa weWHO. Kunesidingo esibonakalayo sokuthi imiphakathi, kanye nabasebenzi bezempilo, bafundiswe kangcono ngokunikela nokufakelwa futhi lokho kuwukhiye wempumelelo yezinhlelo zokunikela abashonile. Nokho, naphezu kokusetshenziswa njalo kwezinto ezinikelwa kubanikeli abashonile, iminikelo evela kubanikeli abaphilayo iyadingeka kwezinye izinhlobo zokufakelwa noma ukunxephezela ukunikezwa okulinganiselwe kwezinto ezitholakala kubanikeli abashonile ukuze kuhlangatshezwane nezidingo zesiguli. Ukunikela okuphilayo kwenziwa kanjalo naphezu kweqiniso lokuthi kuhilela izingozi kumnikeli okungenzeka azinakwa. Njengoba ukuthengwa kwezinto ezisetshenziswa abantu zokufakelwa kabusha kusuka kubanikeli abashonile noma abaphilayo kanye nokufakelwa kabusha kwe-allogeneic okulandelayo kungase kufake izingozi zezimiso zokuziphatha nezokuphepha kubo bobabili umamukeli kanye nomnikeli, ukulawulwa okuqinile nokwengamela okusebenzayo kufanele kwenziwe iziphathimandla zezempilo ukuze zibavikele.
translations.reference: the transplantation of haematopoietic stem cells can cure congenital or acquired diseases including some leukaemias See further information on Haematopoeitic Stem Cells the transplantation of a human heart valve often constitutes the best replacement situation and recipient patients do not require long term anti-coagulation therapy. Xenotransplantation Xenotransplantation, animal to human, defined as living cells, tissues or organs of animal origin and human body fluids, cells, tissues or organs that have ex vivo with these living, xenogeneic materials, has the potential to constitute an alternative to material of human origin and bridge the shortfall in human material for transplantation. Donation: Donations from deceased persons should be developed to their maximum therapeutic potential avoiding, whenever possible, the inherent risks to live donors, as stated in WHO Guiding Principle 3. There is a recognized need for communities, and health professionals, to become better educated about donation and transplantation and that is the key to the success of deceased donation programmes. However, despite the frequent use of materials donated from deceased donors, the donations from living donors are necessary for some types of transplants or to compensate for the limited supply of material available from deceased donors in order to meet patient needs. Living donation is thus practised despite the fact that it involves risks for the donor that may not be negligible. As the procurement of human material for transplantation from deceased or living donors and the subsequent allogeneic transplantation may entail ethical and safety risks for both the recipient and the donor, strict controls and effective oversight should be carried out by the health authorities to protect them.

Xenotransplantation, animal to human, which is defined as living cells, tissues or organs of animal origin and human body fluids, cells, tissues or organs with ex vivo these living materials, xenogeneic, have the potential to create another human origin and to close the shortage of materials used for re-transplantation. However, despite the constant use of materials from deceased donors, donations from living donors are required in some types of transplantation or to supplement the limited availability of materials from deceased donors in order to meet patient needs. Living donation is therefore carried out despite the fact that it entails risks for the donor that cannot be avoided. As the procurement of materials used for re-transplantation comes from deceased or living donors and subsequent allogeneic re-transplantation may involve risks of ethical and safety principles for both the recipient and the donor, strict and enforceable regulation should be implemented by health authorities to protect them."

translations.source: Izimiso Eziqondisayo zigunyaza ukunakekelwa okufanele kubanikeli nabamukeli. Ukwengamela okusobala kweziphathimandla zezempilo mayelana neminikelo kanye nemisebenzi yokufakelwa kabusha kubalulekile futhi ukuze kwandiswe ukwethenjwa komphakathi ohlelweni. Ukwengeza, isinqumo sokuba umnikeli ngokuvamile sisekelwe ekuqondeni ukuthi umnikelo ekutholakaleni kwezinsiza zokufakelwa kabusha kungase ngolunye usuku kuzuze izidingo zezempilo zomndeni walowo onikelayo. Uma sikulungele ukwamukela ukufakelwa kabusha uma sikudinga, kufanele sikulungele ukunikeza Izimiso Zokuziphatha: Izimiso Eziqondisayo zeWHO Kungqamuzana Lomuntu, Izicubu Nezitho Zokufakelwa Ngo-1987 iWorld Health Assembly yamashumi amane, ephathelene nokuhweba ngenzuzo ezithweni zomuntu, yaqala ukulungiswa kweMigomo Yokuqondisa YeWHO Yokutshala Utshani, eyagunyazwa nguMkhandlu ngo-1991 ngesinqumo esithi WHA44.25. Le Migomo Eqondisayo ibe nomthelela omkhulu kumakhodi nezinqubo zochwepheshe kanye nemithetho emhlabeni wonke cishe phakathi neminyaka engamashumi amabili. Ngemva kwenqubo yokubonisana eyathatha iminyaka eminingana, Umhlangano Wezempilo Womhlaba Wamashumi Ayisithupha Nantathu wamukela isinqumo esithi WHA63.22 mhla zingama-21 kuNhlaba wezi-2010, esigunyaza Izimiso Zokuqondisa zeWHO ezibuyekeziwe kanye nokuhlonza izindawo zenqubekelaphambili ukuze kuthuthukiswe izinqubo zokunikela nokutshala. Isimemezelo seIstanbul Ochwepheshe bomnikelo nokufakelwa kabusha abavela kuzo zonke izifunda, ngokusebenzisa izinhlangano zabo eziningi nezikhungo, bagunyaza Isimemezelo sase-Istanbul mayelana nokushushumbiswa kwezitho zomzimba kanye nezokuvakasha zokufakelwa kabusha ezakhiwe ngaphansi kobuholi beTransplantation Society kanye ne-International Society of Nephrology ngoNhlaba 2008.
translations.reference: The Guiding Principles mandate optimal care for donors and recipients. The transparent oversight of the health authorities over donation and transplantation activities is also essential to increase the trust of the public in the system. In addition, the decision to be a donor is often based on the understanding that a contribution to the availability of transplant resources may someday benefit the health needs of the donor’s family. If we are prepared to receive a transplant should we need one, then we should be ready to give Ethics: WHO Guiding Principles on Human Cell, Tissue and Organ Transplantation In 1987 the fortieth World Health Assembly, concerned at the trade for profit in human organs, initiated the preparation of the first WHO Guiding Principles on Transplantation, endorsed by the Assembly in 1991 in resolution WHA44.25. These Guiding Principles have greatly influenced professional codes and practices as well as legislation around the world during almost two decades. After a consultation process that took several years, the Sixty-third World Health Assembly adopted resolution WHA63.22 on 21 May 2010, endorsing the updated WHO Guiding Principles and identifying areas of progress to optimize donation and transplantation practices. The Declaration of Istanbul Professionals of donation and transplantation from all regions, through many of their organizations and institutions, are endorsing the Declaration of Istanbul on organ trafficking and transplant tourism developed under the leadership of the Transplantation Society and the International Society of Nephrology in May 2008.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Zulu document into English:

Guiding Principles authorize appropriate care for both donors and recipients. Clear advocacy by health professionals regarding donations and transplantation procedures is essential and to enhance public trust in the system. Furthermore, the decision to donate is often based on the understanding that a donation of transplantable resources may one day benefit the health needs of the donor’s family. When we are willing to accept transplantation when we need it, we should be willing to give. The WHO’s Principles of Ethics: Guiding Principles on Human Organs, Tissues and Cells for Transplantation – 1987, adopted by the Fortieth World Health Assembly, concerning the profit-making trade in human organs, initiated the revision of the WHO’s Guiding Principles on Organ Transplantation, which was adopted by the Executive Board in 1991 by resolution WHA44.25. These Guiding Principles have had a significant impact on codes and professional practices and laws worldwide for almost two decades. Following a consultation process that took several years, the Sixty-Third World Health Assembly adopted resolution WHA63.22 on 21 May 2010, endorsing the revised WHO Guiding Principles and identifying areas for progress to improve donation and transplantation processes. The Istanbul Declaration Transplantation professionals from all regions, through their various organizations and centers, endorsed the Istanbul Declaration on the prevention of trafficking in human organs and transplant tourism, built under the leadership of the Transplantation Society and the International Society of Nephrology in November 2008.

translations.source: Ukuhamba nempilo Uhlolojikelele: Ukuvakasha kwamanye amazwe kungabeka engcupheni ehlukahlukene empilweni, kuye ngezici zombili zomhambi kanye nohambo. Abahambi bangase bahlangabezane nezinguquko ezingazelelwe neziphawulekayo endaweni, umswakama, amagciwane, nezinga lokushisa, okungase kubangele impilo ebuthaka. Ukwengeza, izingozi ezingathi sína zezempilo zingase ziphakame ezindaweni lapho indawo yokuhlala isezingeni eliphansi, inhlanzeko kanye nokukhucululwa kwendle kunganele, izinsizakalo zezokwelapha azithuthukisiwe kahle futhi amanzi ahlanzekile engatholakali. Ukuvakasha kwamazwe ngamazwe kuyanda, njengoba izigidi zabantu zihamba ngezinjongo zobungcweti, zezenhlalo, zokungcebeleka kanye nokusiza abantu unyaka ngamunye. Bonke abahambi kufanele balungiselele inhlobonhlobo yezingozi zezempilo abangachayeka kuzo ezindaweni abangazazi ngaphambi, ngesikhathi nangemuva kohambo. Abantu abahlela ukuvakasha kufanele bafune iseluleko mayelana nezingozi ezingase zibe khona ezindaweni abazikhethile futhi baqonde indlela engcono kakhulu yokuvikela impilo yabo kanye nokunciphisa ubungozi bokuthola izifo. Ukuhlela phambili, izinyathelo ezifanele zokuvimbela kanye nezinyathelo zokuqapha ngokucophelela kungavikela impilo yabahambi, futhi kunciphise ubungozi bengozi kanye nokuthola izifo. Nakuba umkhakha wezokwelapha kanye nemboni yezokuvakasha inganikeza usizo olubanzi nezeluleko eziphusile, kusewumthwalo wemfanelo womhambi ukufuna ulwazi, ukuqonda ubungozi obuhilelekile kanye nokuthatha izinyathelo zokuphepha ezidingekayo ukuze avikele impilo yakhe ngenkathi ehamba. Ubungozi: Lapho kunqunywa ubungozi bezempilo bokuvakasha kwamanye amazwe, abahambi kanye nabasebenzi bezempilo kufanele bacabangele lezi zici ezibalulekile ezilandelayo:
translations.reference: Travel and health Overview: International travel can pose various risks to health, depending on the characteristics of both the traveller and the travel. Travellers may encounter sudden and significant changes in altitude, humidity, microbes, and temperature, which can result in ill-health. In addition, serious health risks may arise in areas where accommodation is of poor quality, hygiene and sanitation are inadequate, medical services are not well developed and clean water is unavailable. International travel is on the rise, as millions of people travel for professional, social, recreational and humanitarian purposes each year. All travellers must prepare for the variety of health risks they can be exposed to in unfamiliar environments before, during and after they travel. People planning to travel should seek advice on the potential hazards in their chosen destinations and understand how best to protect their health and minimize the risk of acquiring disease. Forward planning, appropriate preventive measures and careful precautions can protect the health of travellers, and minimize the risks of accident and of acquiring disease. Although the medical profession and the travel industry can provide extensive help and sound advice, it remains the traveller’s responsibility to seek information, to understand the risks involved and to take the necessary precautions to protect their health while travelling. Risk: When determining the health risks of international travel, travellers and health professionals should consider the following key factors:
translations.hypothesis:

**Travel and Health: An Overview**

International travel can expose individuals to a variety of health risks, depending on both the traveler’s characteristics and the nature of the trip. Travelers may encounter unexpected and significant changes in environment, altitude, microbes, and temperature, which can lead to ill health. Furthermore, various health hazards may be heightened in areas where living conditions are substandard, sanitation and environmental control are inadequate, healthcare services are poorly developed, and clean water is unavailable. International travel is increasing, with billions of people traveling for professional, social, recreational, and humanitarian purposes each year. All travelers should prepare for the range of health risks they may encounter in unfamiliar locations before, during, and after travel. People planning to travel should seek advice about potential hazards in their chosen destinations and understand the best ways to protect their health and reduce the risk of contracting diseases. Careful planning, appropriate preventive measures, and vigilant monitoring can protect the health of travelers and minimize the risk of accidents and disease acquisition. While the medical sector and the tourism industry can provide broad assistance and expert advice, it remains the traveler’s responsibility to seek information, understand the risks involved, and take the necessary safety precautions to protect their health while traveling. Risks: When assessing the health risks of international travel, travelers and healthcare workers should consider the following important factors:

translations.source: izinguquko zemvelo, njengokuphakama, umswakama, izinga lokushisa izifo ezibangelwa ukudla kanye namanzi izinsiza zezokwelapha ezinganele ukulimala, okuhlanganisa izingozi zomgwaqo noma imisebenzi yokuzijabulisa impilo engokwengqondo, okuhlanganisa ukucindezeleka kanye nokushaqeka kwamasiko Abahambi bangase bachayeke ezifweni eziningi ezithathelwanayo, futhi eziningi zazo zingavinjelwa ngokugoma. Kodwa ke, kunezifo ezithathelwanayo, kuhlanganise nezinye eziyingozi kakhulu, ezingenayo imithi yokugoma. Izindawo lapho indawo yokuhlala, inhlanzeko nokukhucululwa kwendle, ukunakekelwa kwezempilo kanye nekhwalithi yamanzi kusezingeni eliphezulu kubangela ubungozi obuncane obumbalwa empilweni yabahambi. Lapho lezi zinsizakalo zinganikezwanga, abahambi kufanele bathathe izinyathelo eziqinile zokugwema ukugula. Izixwayiso zokuhamba ezivela emithonjeni kahulumeni kufanele zithathwe ngokungathi sína.
translations.reference: environmental changes, such as altitude, humidity, temperature foodborne and waterborne diseases inadequate medical services injuries, including from road traffic accidents or recreational activities psychological health, including stress and culture shock Travellers may also be exposed to a number of infectious diseases, and many of these can be prevented by vaccination. However, there are some infectious diseases, including some of the most dangerous, for which no vaccines exist. Destinations where accommodation, hygiene and sanitation, medical care and water quality are of a high standard pose relatively few serious risks to the health of travellers. Where these services are not provided, travellers should take stringent precautions to avoid illness. Travel warnings from governmental sources should be taken seriously.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Zulu document into English:

"Environmental changes, such as altitude, humidity, temperature, foodborne and waterborne illnesses, inadequate medical resources, injuries, including traffic accidents or recreational activities, and mental health issues, including stress and culture shock. Travelers may be exposed to many interconnected diseases, and many of these can be prevented with caution. However, there are interconnected diseases, including some of the most dangerous, that have no cure. Areas where living conditions, hygiene, environmental conservation, healthcare, and water quality are at a high level cause fewer health risks for travelers. When these services are not provided, travelers should take strict measures to avoid illness. Travel warnings from government sources should be taken seriously."

translations.source: Ukuvimbela: Ngaphambi kohambo: Thintana nesikhungo semithi yokuhamba noma udokotela, okungcono amasonto ama-4-8 ngaphambi kokuhamba ukuze uthole noma yimiphi imigomo edingekayo; funda ngezifo zendawo ezihlobene nendawo oya kuyo; cela ulwazi ngengozi kamalaleveva kanye nokuvimbela ukulunywa omiyane. Lokhu kungase kuhlanganise ukuthenga imithi yokuvimbela efanele kanye nokupakisha inethi kanye nomuthi oxosha izinambuzane; thola ukuhlolwa kodokotela kanye namazinyo; thola imiyalelo yemithi ngokuya ngobude besikhathi sokuhlala futhi uhlanganise isethi yezokwelapha efanele; ukuthenga umshwalense wezokwelapha ngekhava efanele phesheya Phakathi nohambo: dla kuphela ukudla okuphekwe kahle futhi uphuze kuphela iziphuzo ezibandayo ezisemabhodleleni noma ezipakishiwe. Bilisa amanzi okuphuza uma ukuphepha kuthandabuza.
translations.reference: Prevention: Before travel: contact a travel medicine centre or a physician, preferably 4–8 weeks before departure to receive any required vaccinations; read up on local diseases related to your destination; request information on malaria risk and prevention of mosquito bites. This may include purchasing appropriate preventive medication and packing a bednet and insect repellent; get a medical and dental check-up; obtain prescriptions for medication according to length of stay and assemble a suitable medical kit; purchase medical insurance with appropriate cover abroad During travel: eat only thoroughly cooked food and drink only bottled or packaged cold drinks. Boil drinking-water if safety is doubtful.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Zulu document into English:

**Prevention:** Before traveling: Consult a travel clinic or doctor, preferably 4-8 weeks before traveling to obtain any necessary vaccinations; learn about local diseases related to the area you are going to; ask for information about blood clots and malaria prevention. This may include purchasing appropriate preventative medication and mosquito nets and insect repellent; get a medical and dental check-up; obtain prescriptions for medication according to the length of stay and assemble a suitable medical kit; purchase travel insurance with adequate coverage abroad. During travel: Only eat well-cooked food and only drink chilled beverages from bottles or cans. Boil drinking water if safety is questionable.
