Editor's note: Eye disease (trachoma) will remain a public health problem in 41 countries (as of July 2022), affecting an estimated 125 million people living in areas needing intervention for this disease."

translations.source: Isifo samehlo sitholakala kakhulu ezindaweni ezingamakhaya futhi ezihluphekayo e-Afrika, Maphakathi naseNingizimu neAmerica, Asia naseWestern Pacific kanye neMiddle East. I-WHO African Region itheleleke yisifo samehlo kubantu abayizigidi ezingu 105 abahlala ezindaweni eziyingcuphe,ezimele u-48% womthwalo wesifo samehlo emhlabeni. Isifo samehlo isona esihamba phambili ekuthelelaneni esidala ukungaboni emhlabeni wonke. Isifo sidalwa ukuthelelana ngegciwane iChlamydia trachomatis. Ukuthelelana kudluliseka kumuntu kuye kumuntu ngeminwe engcolile, izinambuzane, izimpukane esezihlangabezane nokungcola okuphuma emahlweni noma ikhala lomuntu othelelekile. Izinkinga zengcephe yendawo yokudluliselwa kwesifo samehlo okufaka ukungahlanzeki,imizi egcwele ukungatholi kahle amanzi nokusebenzisa izindawo zokuzikhulula ezifanele. Ngo-1996, iWHO yethula iWHO Alliane yokuqedwa kwesifo samehlo emhlabeni ngonyaka ka-2020 (GET2020). Nabanye ababambisani kwi-Alliance, iWHO yeseka ukwenza kwezwe kweSu lokuPhepha nokuqinisa amandla ezwe ngokhlolwa kwezobhubhane, ukubheka, ukuhlola, ukubhekisa iphrojekthi nokuhlanganisa izinsizakusebenza ezizosiza ekuqedweni kwesifo samehlo njengenkinga yezempilo yomphakathi. Umgwaqo balazwe ogunyazwe nguMhlangano weZempilo eMhlabeni ngo-2020 ngesinqumo sawo 73(33) ibeka u-2030 njengosuku oluqondiwe olusha lokuqeda emhlabeni.
translations.reference: Trachoma is found mainly in the poorest and most rural areas of Africa, Central and South America, Asia, the Western Pacific and the Middle East. The WHO African Region is disproportionately affected by trachoma with 105 million people living in at-risk areas, which represents 84% of the global trachoma burden. Trachoma is the leading infectious cause of blindness worldwide. The disease is caused by infection with the bacterium Chlamydia trachomatis. Infection is transmitted from person to person through contaminated fingers, fomites and flies that have come into contact with discharge from the eyes or nose of an infected person. Environmental risk factors for trachoma transmission include poor hygiene, overcrowded households, inadequate access to water or use of proper sanitation facilities. In 1996, WHO launched the WHO Alliance for the Global Elimination of Trachoma by the year 2020 (GET2020). With other partners in the Alliance, WHO supports country implementation of the SAFE strategy and strengthening of national capacity through epidemiological assessment, monitoring, surveillance, project evaluation and resource mobilization contributing towards elimination of trachoma as a public health problem. The road map, endorsed by the World Health Assembly in 2020 through its decision 73(33), sets 2030 as the new target date for global elimination.

Eye disease is predominantly found in impoverished and vulnerable areas of Africa, Central and Southern America, Asia, the Western Pacific, and the Middle East. The WHO African Region is burdened by eye disease affecting 105 million people living in high-risk areas, representing 48% of the global burden of eye disease. Eye disease is the leading cause of preventable blindness worldwide. The disease is caused by infection with the bacterium Chlamydia trachomatis. Infection is transmitted from person to person through contaminated fingers, insects, or cloths that have come into contact with discharge from the eyes or tears of an infected person. Risk factors for the transmission of eye disease include poor hygiene, communities lacking access to clean water, and the use of inadequate sanitation facilities. In 1996, the WHO launched the WHO Alliance for the Elimination of Trachoma Globally by 2020 (GET2020). Together with other partners in the Alliance, WHO supports national efforts to strengthen health security through disease surveillance, assessment, investigation, project referral, and the integration of services to help eliminate eye disease as a public health problem. The global roadmap endorsed by the World Health Assembly in 2020 through resolution 73(33) sets 2030 as the new global target for elimination.

translations.source: Izigidi ezingu-152 izingane ezizalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi kushuminyaka elidlule Ezinganeni ezingu-10 ezizalwayo, eyodwa izalwa phambi kwesikhathi futhi emizuzwini engu-40 eyodwa kulezo ngane iyafa Amazinga okuzalwa kwezingane ngaphambi kwesikhathi awakashintshi kushuminyaka eledlule kunoma yisiphi isifunda emhlabeni Imithelela yengxabano,ukuguquka kwesimo sezulu,ingcuphe yokunyuka kweCOVID-19 kubantu besifazane nezingane yonke indawo Isu liyadingeka ngokushesha ukwenza ngcono ukuvikela ukuzalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi, okuhambisana nokunakekela okungcono ezinganeni ezikhahlamezekile kanye nemindeni yazo Isilinganiso sezigindi ezingu-13.4 izingane ezizalwe ngaphambi kwesikhathi nokungafika esigidini esisodwa semithelela yokufa, ngokombiko omusha okhishiwe yi-United Nations ama-ejensi kanye nababambisani namhlanje. Lo mzuliswano wokulingana wengane eyodwa kweziyishumi ezisheshe zizalwe (ngaphambi kwamasonto angu-37 okukhulelwa) emhlabeni wonke. Ukusheshe uzalwe: ishuminyaka lokwenza ekuzalweni ngaohambi kwesikhathi, kukhiqizwe Inhlangano yezempilo emhlabeni (WHO) ne United Nations Children Fund (UNICEF) ndawonye nePMNCH -- ubudlelwane obukhulu emhlabeni babantu besifazane, izingane, nentsha, kuveza ukuuthusa bokuthula kokuphuthumayo kwezokuzalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi, okunesikhathi eside kunganakiwe esikalini sakho sobucayi okuveza ukuqhubeka ekwenzeningcono ezempilo zezingane nokuphila. Umbiko ukubuyekezwa kulinganisa okuphuma kuWHO ne-UNICEF, izilungiselele neLondon School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, ekusabalaleni kokuzalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi. Kukhona konke kutholakala ukuthi amazinga okuzalwa kwezingane ngaphambi kwesikhathi awakashintshi kunoma yisiphi isifunda emhlabeni kuleshuminyaka eledlule nezigidi ezingu-152 zezingane ezihluphekile ezisheshe zazalwa kusuka ngo-2010 kuya ku-2020.
translations.reference: 152 million babies born preterm in the last decade Of every 10 babies born, 1 is preterm – and every 40 seconds, 1 of those babies dies Preterm birth rates have not changed in the past decade in any region of the world The impacts of conflict, climate change, COVID-19 are increasing risks for women and babies everywhere Action is needed urgently to improve the prevention of preterm birth, alongside better care for affected babies and their families An estimated 13.4 million babies were born pre-term in 2020, with nearly 1 million dying from preterm complications, according to a new report released by United Nations agencies and partners today. This is equivalent to around 1 in 10 babies born early (before 37 weeks of pregnancy) worldwide. Born too soon: decade of action on preterm birth, produced by the World Health Organization (WHO) and the United Nations Children’s Fund (UNICEF) together with PMNCH -- the world’s largest alliance for women, children, and adolescents, sounds the alarm on a “silent emergency” of preterm birth, long under-recognized in its scale and severity, which is impeding progress in improving children’s health and survival. The report includes updated estimates from WHO and UNICEF, prepared with the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, on the prevalence of preterm births. Overall, it finds that preterm birth rates have not changed in any region in the world in the past decade, with 152 million vulnerable babies born too soon from 2010 to 2020.

"152 million babies were born prematurely last year. Of every 10 babies born, one is born prematurely, and one of those babies dies within 40 minutes. Rates of premature births have not changed in the past year in any region of the world. Conflict, climate change, and the threat of rising COVID-19 cases pose a risk to women and children everywhere. Urgent action is needed to improve the prevention of premature births, along with better care for healthy children and their families. An estimated 13.4 million preterm babies and a staggering one million stillbirths, according to a new report released by United Nations agencies and partners today. This alarming trend shows that one in every ten babies is born prematurely (before 37 weeks of gestation) worldwide. A decade of action on preventing preterm birth, produced jointly by the World Health Organization (WHO) and the United Nations Children’s Fund (UNICEF) with the PMNCH -- the largest global partnership for women’s, children’s and adolescents’ health -- highlights the urgency of addressing the preventable loss of preterm lives, a crisis that has long been overlooked and undermines progress on child health and survival. The report revises estimates based on data from WHO and UNICEF, and prepared with the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, on the prevalence of preterm birth. It finds that rates of preterm birth have not changed in any region of the world in the past year, and an estimated 152 million babies have experienced preterm birth from 2010 to 2020."

translations.source: Ukuzalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi manje sekuyimbangela eholayo ekufeni kwezingane,ukubala okungaphezu kuka 1 wokungu 5 wakho konke ukufa okwenzeka ngaphambi kosuku lokuzalwa lweminyaka emihlanu. Abaphilayo kwabazalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi babhekana nemiphumela yezempilo impilo yabo yonke, ngokukhula kokujwayeleka kokukhubazeka kanye nokubambezeleka kokukhula. Izikhala zokuphila yisifunda, imali engenayo, ibala Ukwakhela emmbikweni wesikhonkwane esihlokweni sango-2012,lokhu kusheshe uzalwe, ishuminyaka, umbiko unikeza ukubhekwa okukhulu kokusabalala kokuzalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi nomthelela oqinile kwabesifazane, imiphakathi nezomnotho. Kujwayelekile lapho izingane zizalelwe khona kuzonquma ukuthi zizophila yini. Umbiko uqaphela ukuthi uyedwa kuphela kwabangu-10 abantwana abasheshe bazalwa (<amasonto angu-28) abaphilayo emzaweni anemali encane engenayo,uma uqhathanisa kwangaphezu kwangu-9 ku-10 emazweni anemali ephezulu engenayo. Izikhala zokungalingani ezihlobene nebala, nobuzwe nemali engenayo nokthola ukunakekelwa okufanele kukhomba ukuphila kokusheshe uzalwe ukufa kanye nokukhubazeka ngisho nasemazweni anemali ephezulu engenayo. Southern Asia nesub-Saharan Afrika banamazinga aphezulu okusheshe kuzalwe kulezizizfunda futhi abhekene nengcuphe yokushona kwezingane ephezulu. Ndawonye, lezi zifunda ezimbili zibala ngaphezu kuka-65% wezingane ezisheshe zazalwa emhlabeni. Umbiko uphinde uveze umthelela wengxabano,ukuguquka kwesimo sezulu nokulimala kwendawo, COVID-19 nokunyuka kokubiza kwempilo okunyusa ingcuphe kubantu besifazane nezingane yonke indawo.
translations.reference: Preterm birth is now the leading cause of child deaths, accounting for more than 1 in 5 of all deaths of children occurring before their 5th birthday. Preterm survivors can face lifelong health consequences, with an increased likelihood of disability and developmental delays. Survival gaps by region, income, race Building from a landmark report on the topic in 2012, this new Born too soon “decade” report provides a comprehensive overview of the prevalence of preterm birth and its profound impact on women, families, societies and economies. Too often, where babies are born determines if they survive. The report notes that only 1 in 10 extremely preterm babies (<28 weeks) survive in low-income countries, compared to more than 9 in 10 in high-income countries. Gaping inequalities related to race, ethnicity, income, and access to quality care determine the likelihood of preterm birth, death, and disability, even in high-income countries. Southern Asia and sub-Saharan Africa have the highest rates of preterm birth, and preterm babies in these regions face the highest mortality risk. Together, these two regions account for more than 65% of preterm births globally. The report also highlights that the impacts of conflict, climate change and environmental damage, COVID-19, and rising living costs are increasing risks for women and babies everywhere.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Zulu document into English:

"Preterm birth is a leading cause of death among children, accounting for more than 1 in 5 of all deaths occurring before the age of five. Those born preterm face health consequences throughout their lives, with an increased risk of disability and developmental delays. Factors influencing survival include location, income, and ethnicity. A report built on data from 2012 onwards, highlighting the rapid increase in preterm births and the significant impact on women, communities, and the economy. Where a child is born often determines whether they will survive. The report notes that 1 in 10 preterm babies (<28 weeks) survive in low-income settings, compared to more than 9 in 10 in high-income countries. Disparities related to ethnicity, country, and income, and access to appropriate care, contribute to the survival of preterm babies and to disability even in high-income countries. Southern Asia and sub-Saharan Africa have high rates of preterm birth and face a high risk of child mortality. Together, these two regions account for more than 65% of preterm births worldwide. The report also highlights the impact of conflict, climate change, environmental degradation, COVID-19, and rising healthcare costs, increasing the risk for women and children everywhere."

translations.source: Isibonelo, ukungcola komoya kulinganiselwa ukuthi kunomthelela wezigidi ezingu-6 zokusheshe kuzalwe unyaka ngamunye. Kungafika ku 1 kwabangu 10 izingane ezisheshe zazalwa ezizalwe emazweni abuthaka angu-10 akhahlamezwe izinkinga zabantu ngokokuhlaziya okusha embikweni. Ingcuphe yomama abakhulelwe, njengokukhulelwa kwamatshitshi kusondelene nokusheshe uzale. Lokhu kugqamisa isidingo sokuqinisekisa ukuthola ezempilo zokuzala nezocansi, okufaka ukusebenza kokuhlela umndeni, ukunakekelwa okusezingeni eliphezulu kwabakhulelwe nangesikhathi sokuzala. Ajenda yokwenza: utshalomali oluningi ezweni nokushoshova okuholwa ngabazali Ishuminyaka eledlulesibone ukukhula kwezishoshovu emphakathini ekuvinjweni kokusheshe kuzalwe kwezingane, okuholwa uhlelokuxhumana lwabazali, izifundiswa zempilo, izifundiswa umphakathi nabanye. Ezweni lonke amaqoqo emindeni ekhahlamezekile ngokusheshe kuzalwe kwengane alokhu ephambili ekumeleni ukuthola ukunakekelwa okungcono nokushinthwa kwenqubomgomo nokweseka imindeni. Okuholela kuInternational Maternal Newborn Helath Conference, eKapa eNingizimu Afrika kusuka ngo-8-11 kuNhlaba, iWHO, i-UNICEF, i-UNFPA nePMNCH bamemezela ukulandela izenzo ukwenza ngcono ukunakekela kwabesifazane nabazelwe ubukhulu bezingcuphe okuvela kokuzalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi. Ukunyuswa kotshalomali:ukuhlanganisa izinsizakusebenza zamazwe angaphandle nezendawo ukusiza ezempilo zomama nezingane ezizelwe, ukuqinisekisa ukunakekelwa okuphezulu kuyanikezwa uma noma nalapho kudingeka khona. Ukunyuswa kokwenza: Ukuhlangabezana nokuqondwe ngamazwe kokuqhubeka ngokwenza okusungulwe yinqubomgomo yezwe kokunakekelwa komama nezingane ezizelwe.
translations.reference: For example, air pollution is estimated to contribute to 6 million preterm births each year. Nearly 1 in 10 preterm babies are born in the 10 most fragile countries affected by humanitarian crises, according to a new analysis in the report. Maternal health risks, such as adolescent pregnancy and pre-eclampsia, are closely linked to preterm births. This underlines the need to ensure access to sexual and reproductive health services, including effective family planning, with high-quality care in pregnancy and around the time of birth. Agenda for action: more country investment and parent-led activism The past decade has also seen a growth of community activism on preterm birth and stillbirth prevention, driven by networks of parents, health professionals, academia, civil society, and others. Throughout the world, groups for affected families of preterm birth have been at the forefront of advocating for access to better care and policy change and supporting other families. Leading up to the International Maternal Newborn Health Conference, in Cape Town, South Africa from 8-11 May, WHO, UNICEF, UNFPA and PMNCH are calling for the following actions to improve care for women and newborns and mitigate risks from preterm births: Increased investments: Mobilizing international and domestic resources to optimize maternal and newborn health, ensuring high quality care is provided when and where it is needed. Accelerated implementation: Meeting country targets for progress through implementing established national policies for maternal and newborn care.

Up to 1 in 10 of these rapidly born children are born in 10 vulnerable countries facing humanitarian crises, according to new analysis in a report. The risk to pregnant mothers, such as teenage pregnancy, is closely linked to rapid childbirth. This highlights the need to ensure access to reproductive health services, including family planning, quality care for pregnant women and during childbirth. Agenda for action: increased investment in the country and parental support. Last year saw growth in initiatives within communities to prevent adolescent pregnancy, driven by parental communication, health educators, community health workers, and others. Across the globe, groups of families affected by rapid childbirth are at the forefront of advocating for better care and policy changes to support families. Leading up to the International Maternal Newborn Health Conference, in Cape Town, South Africa from 8-11 November, WHO, UNICEF, UNFPA and PMNCH announced follow-up actions to improve the care of women and newborns facing risks from preterm birth. Increased investment: integrating international and local resources to support maternal and newborn health services, ensuring quality care is provided whenever and wherever it is needed. Increased action: Meeting and understanding national commitments to continue the work established by national policies on maternal and newborn health."

translations.reference: Integration across sectors: Promoting education through the life cycle; supporting smarter economic investments, with co-financing across sectors; strengthening climate adaptation responses across the life-course; and advancing the coordination and resilience of emergency systems. Locally driven innovation: Investing in locally led innovation and research to support improvements in quality of care and equity in access. Quotes: Prof. Joy Lawn, London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine Co-lead, Born too soon: decade of action on preterm birth (2023) and Born too soon (2012) "This new report shows that the cost of inaction over the last decade was 152 million babies born too soon. While some regions are more affected, preterm birth threatens health progress in every country. Greater investment in the care of vulnerable newborns can save millions of families from heartbreak. More work is also needed to prevent preterm birth, which will also improve progress in reducing stillbirths and maternal deaths. Together these twin tracks of preterm prevention and care will produce healthier individuals and societies to deliver on economic and social development. Our next generation depends on us all acting now -- the investment may not be small but the returns on this investment will be major for every country"

Progress is also needed to help prevent preterm births -- this means every woman must be able to access quality health services before and during pregnancy to identify and manage risks.” Steven Lauwerier, Director of Health (a.i.), UNICEF “After every preterm death is a trail of loss and heartbreak.

translations.source: Ngaphandle kobungcono okuningi emhlabeni esikwenzile eshumininyaka eledlule, asikenzi nqubekela phambili ekwehliseni inamba yezingane ezincane ezisheshe zizalwe noma sivimbe ingcuphe ekuzalweni kwabo. Loluhambo luyakhubaza. Yisikhathi sokwenza ngcono ukuthola ukunakekelwa komama abakhulelwe kanye nezingane ezizalwe kungashayi isikhathi nokuqinisekisa ukuthi yonke ingane ithola ukuqala kwezempilo nokuphokophela empilweni." UHelga Fogstad, uMqondisi Omkhulu, wePMNCH Ukusheshe uzalwe kusho incazelo yokuthi kumele sinyuse utshalomali nesibophezelo kwabasheshe bazalwa - imbangela enkulu emhlabeni yokufa kwezingane ezingaphansi kweminyaka emihlanu. Ukuqhubeka kokulinganisa kumama nezempilo zomntwana ozelwe, kanye nokuvikelwa kokuzalwa kwabantwana kungakashayi isikhathi, manje sekubekwe emuva ngokuhlanganiswa okukhathazayo kweCOVID-19, ukuguquka kwesimo sezulu, ukwandisa izingxabano nokukhulisa izindleko zokuphila. Ngokusebenza ndawonye ngokubambisana - uhulumeni, abaxhasi, umkhakha ozimele, umphakathi, abazali, izifundiswa zempilo - singaveza inkinga ngokuthula kokuphuthuma nokuletha ukuvikelwa kokuzalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi nemizamo yokunakelewa phambili kwezempilo yezwe nemizamo yokuthuthukisa, ukwakha amandla omuntu ngokweseka imindeni, imiphakathi nezomnotho yonke indawo. "UGabriela noJerome Foster, e-Las Vegas, e-United States: abazali baJalen Foster, abasunguli beJalen Gift Foundation "Indawo yethu yasungulwa ngemva kokushona komfana wethu, uJalen Foster, owasheshe wazalwa enamasonto angu-27 ngenxa yokusheshe ngisikwe ngegciwane elingaziwa njengeqoqo B streptococcus. Ukulahlekelwa yindodana yethu kwasifakela ingcindezi futhi kwakungekho ukwesekwa emphakathini nokuthi ngesikhathi sinquma ukunikezela ngendawo ukududuza nokunika ithemba emindenini elahlekelwe ngumntwana.
translations.reference: Despite the many advances the world has made in the past decade, we have made no progress in reducing the number of small babies born too soon or averting the risk of their death. The toll is devasting. It’s time we improve access to care for pregnant mothers and preterm infants and ensure every child gets a healthy start and thrives in life.” Helga Fogstad, Executive Director, PMNCH “Born too soon underlines why we must increase investment and accountability for preterm birth – the world’s largest cause of death of children under the age of five. Progress is flatlining for maternal and newborn health, as well as the prevention of stillbirths, and is now pushed back further through the devastating combination of COVID-19, climate change, expanding conflicts and rising living costs. “By working together in partnership – governments, donors, the private sector, civil society, parents, and health professionals – we can sound the alarm about this “silent emergency”, and bring preterm prevention and care efforts to the forefront of national health and development efforts, building human capital by supporting families, societies and economies everywhere. ”Gabriela and Jerome Foster, Las Vegas, United States: parents of Jalen Foster, and founders of Jalen’s Gift Foundation “Our foundation was founded after the loss of our son, Jalen Foster, who was a preterm stillbirth at 27 weeks due to prenatal onset of a bacteria known as group B streptococcus. Losing our son was devastating, and there was no support in the community and that’s when we decided to provide a place of comfort and hope for families who have lost a baby.
translations.hypothesis: Despite the significant progress the world has made in the past few years, we are not making enough headway in reducing the number of babies born too soon or preventing stillbirths. This journey is frustrating. It is time to improve access to timely care for pregnant mothers and newborns and ensure that every baby receives essential health checks and immunization. "Helga Fogstad, Director, PMNCH. Being born too soon means we must increase investment and commitment to preterm babies – a major cause of child mortality under five years of age. Continued progress for mothers and newborn health, and the protection of newborns, is now being set back by the worrying combination of COVID-19, climate change, increasing conflicts, and rising living costs. By working together collaboratively – governments, donors, the private sector, civil society, parents, health professionals – we can address the problem with urgency and bring protection for newborns forward and complement national health efforts and development initiatives, building human resilience by supporting families, communities, and economies everywhere. "Gabriela and Jerome Foster, Las Vegas, United States: parents of Jalen Foster, founders of the Jalen Gift Foundation." Our foundation was established after the death of our son, Jalen Foster, who was born prematurely at 27 weeks due to an infection known as Group B streptococcus. Losing our son put us under immense pressure, and there was no support in the community, and when we decided to establish a foundation to comfort and give hope to families who have lost a child.

translations.source: I-UEA neWHO ihambise iziphakamisi moya zamasaplayi ezokwelapha ezibucayi eSudan United Arab Emirates (UAE) neNhlangano yeZempilo eMhlabeni (WHO) bahambise amathoni angu-30 amasaplayi ezokwelapha okuphuthumayo eSudan namhlanje. Ibhanoyi eliphethe amasaplayi yokwelapha abalimele, ukuhlinza okuphuthumayokanye nezidakamizwa eziphuthumayo lifike isikhumulweni samabhanoyi iPort Sudan ekuseni kakhulu. Ukuhanjswa kwenani elingu US$ 444 000, eyokuqala iWHO ekwazile ukuyihambisa ngezomoya iya eSudan kusukela kube nokubheduka kwengxabano. I-WHO ihambise amasaplayi ezindaweni zempilo ngaphambi kokunyuka kwengxabano. Babekhathele ngemva kwezinsuku ezimbalwa uma ubheka inamba yabalimele. Ukukhishwa kwendizamshini kuza njengengxenye ye-UAE imizamo yokuqhubeka nokukhulula ekwesekeni abantu baseSudanese nokuveza ubuhlobo obunzulu neSudan, ikakhulukazi ngokwesimo samanje esibhekene nezwe. Ukukhululwa kuphinde kube ukululwa kobuntu be-UAE umbono nesikalo sobudlelwano namanye amazwe ancike kubantu nouzibophezela kokusiza abanye ngezikhathi zezinkinga nezimo eziphuthumayo. UDkt Reem bint Ebrahim Al Hashimy, Unqongqoshe wezwe leInternational Cooperation, uthe i-UAE izoqhubeka nokusebenzisana neNhlangano yezempilo emhlabeni ukuqinisekisa ukubekwa okuyimpumelelo kokusebenza kwelojistikhi ebucayi nokuthumela ezokwelapha ngokuphuthuma nosizo lokudla ukusiza ukubhekana nenkinga yamanje eSudan. Nokuhambisana nesimemezelo kuzona zonke izinngxenye zengxabano yamanje ukuyeka manje unya nokuqhuba isimo esidala ukuhlupheka okungashiwo kubantu baseSudan, i-UAE isheshile ekuzibophezelei kwayo ukunikeza usizo nokusiza mazwe ngesikhathi beludinga.
translations.reference: UAE and WHO deliver air lift of critical medical supplies to Sudan The United Arab Emirates (UAE) and the World Health Organization have delivered 30 tons of urgent medical supplies to Sudan today. A plane carrying supplies for injury treatment, emergency surgeries, and essential drugs arrived in Port Sudan Airport early this morning. The shipment, valued at US$ 444 000, is the first that WHO has been able to deliver by air to Sudan since the outbreak of the conflict. WHO distributed supplies to heath facilities prior to the escalation of conflict. These were exhausted after a few days given the number of injured. The dispatch of the aircraft comes as part of the UAE's continuous relief efforts in support of the Sudanese people and reflects its deep relations with Sudan, particularly in light of the current situation the country is facing. The relief is also an extension of the UAE’s humanitarian vision and underscores its relations with other countries based on human fraternity and its commitment to assisting others during crises and emergency situations. Dr Reem bint Ebrahim Al Hashimy, Minister of State for International Cooperation, said that the UAE "continues to work closely alongside the World Health Organization to ensure the successful deployment of the critical logistics operations to send urgent medical and food aid to help address the current crisis in Sudan. In parallel with calls for all sides of the current conflict to immediately cease hostilities and de-escalate a situation that is causing untold suffering for the Sudanese people, the UAE is steadfast in its commitment to providing aid and assistance to countries in times of need.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Zulu document:

The UAE and WHO deliver critical medical supplies to Sudan

The United Arab Emirates (UAE) and the World Health Organization (WHO) have delivered 30 tons of emergency medical supplies to Sudan today. The consignment containing bandages, filters for rapid treatment, and rapid-acting medications arrived at the Port Sudan airport early this morning. The delivery, valued at US$ 444,000, is the first the WHO has been able to air-deliver to Sudan since the outbreak of conflict. The WHO had delivered medical supplies to health facilities before the escalation of the conflict. They were overwhelmed after just a few days considering the number of casualties. The dispatch comes as part of the UAE’s efforts to continue providing support to the people of Sudan and to demonstrate a deep-rooted relationship with Sudan, especially in light of the current situation facing the country. The release is also a reflection of the UAE’s humanitarian vision and commitment to building relationships with other countries that are dedicated to helping others during times of crisis and emergencies. Dr. Reem bint Ebrahim Al Hashimy, Minister of International Cooperation, said that the UAE will continue to cooperate with the World Health Organization to ensure the successful deployment of critical logistics and the rapid delivery of medical and food aid to help address the current situation in Sudan. In line with the call for a cessation of all hostilities in the current conflict to allow for a return to normalcy and alleviate the suffering of the people of Sudan, the UAE has expedited its commitment to providing assistance and supporting nations when they are in need.

translations.source: I-UAE igxile kakhulu ekunikezeni amaqoqo abuthaka kakhulu akhahlamezekile kulesisimo saseSudan, ikakhulukazi abagulayo, izingane,nabadala nabesifazane abasengcupheni yokuqhubeka kwengxabano - futhi lamabhanoyi akhululayo azobhekana ngqo nezikhala eziphuthumayo kwezokulapha kanye nokuhlinzekwa ngokudla. I-UAE igcine ngokujula ubuntu kusho ukuthi isiqinisekisile iyaqhubeka nokuxhumana nokumhlaba okukhulu nokuzinikela kokuqinisa ukuthula, ukuphepha nozinzo, ayi esizindeni kuphela kodwa emhlabeni wonke ngesikhathi nababambisani nomphakathi wamazwe angaphandle, izwe liyaqhubeka nokusiza iSudanese ezikhathini ezinzima. Amasaplayi ezempiloafaka ukuthuka okwanele, amasaplayi okuhlinza okuphuthumayo nemithi ebalulekile ukuthi ifike manje kubantu abangu-165 000 abadinga kakhulu usizo lobuntu. Izindawo zezempilo ezweni lonke zibika ukuthi seziphelelwe masaplayi ayisisekelo sezempilo nokuthi izitolo zokwelapha zezwe akusafinyeleki kuzona ngenxa yezokuphepha ezweni. Kokunye ukwesekwa, iWHO izobeka amalojistikhi amabili okuphuthumayo ukuhamba ngebhanoyi ukuqinisekisa lamasaplayi asehanjiswa ezindaweni zempilo ezinkulu ezingu-13 ukweseka abasebenzi bezempilo nokwengeza ukunakekela kulabo abakudingayo. WHO inamathoni amakhulu angu-30 amasaplayi amalalwveva nezifo okungaxhunyanwa ngazo njengehayihayi noshukela, izimo ezingabulala uma zishiywa zingalashiwe. Lokhu nezikhwama zegazi ezingu-23 000 sezilungisiwe nelojistikhi yomhlaba yeWHO uhabhu kuInternational Humanitarian City neWHO njengamanje ibheka zonke izindlela zokuletha lamasaplayi eSudan ngokushesha ekubambisaneni noNgqongqoshe weZempilo eSudan. UDkt Michael Ryan, uMqondisi omkhulu wohlelo lweziphuthumayo zempilo weWHO, uthe Iwho Global Logistic Hub e-UAE isibe okubalulekile kokuphindeka kwempendulo yesaplayi esigamekweni ezibucayi emhlabeni. Ukweseka amazwe angaphezu kuka-140 kuzona zonke izindawo zezifunda zweWHO, ihabhu inikezela ngempilo yonke yeMember States kusipiliyoni sokuphuthumayo kwezempilo okuvelayo ekubhedukeni kwezifo, izinhlekelele njengezikhukhula nokuzamazama komhlaba, nomthelela wengxabano ezinhlelweni zempilo. Ukwesekwa okunikeziwe yi-UAE ivumele iWHO ukuthi ibe phambili ekuphenduleni kweziphuthumayo zempilo-ukuhambisa usizo longoti bezokwelapha kulabo abadingayo lapho kudingeka khona.
translations.reference: The UAE is particularly focused on providing for the most vulnerable groups affected by the situation in Sudan, especially the sick, children, the elderly, and women who are most at risk from the ongoing concerning conflict – and these relief flights will directly address the most pressing gaps in medical and food provision. The UAE's deeply held humanitarian values have meant that it has ensured it has continued to communicate to the world its robust and unrelenting dedication to strengthening peace, security and stability, not just regionally, but also worldwide, whilst alongside its partners and the international community, the country continues to assist the Sudanese people in times of crisis.” The health supplies include enough trauma, emergency surgical supplies, and essential medicines to immediately reach 165 000 people who are in desperate need of humanitarian aid. Health facilities across the country report they have run out of basic health supplies and that national medical stores are no longer accessible due to the security situation. Among other support, WHO is deploying two emergency logisticians travelling with the cargo to ensure that these supplies are immediately distributed to 13 major health facilities to support healthcare workers and extend care to those in need. WHO has another 30 metric tons of supplies for malaria and noncommunicable diseases like diabetes and hypertension, conditions that can become deadly if left untreated. These and about 23 000 blood bags are being readied within the WHO global logistics hub in the International Humanitarian City, and WHO is currently exploring all possibilities for delivering these supplies to Sudan as quickly as possible in collaboration with the Sudanese Ministry of Health. Dr Michael Ryan, Executive Director, of the WHO Health Emergencies Programme, said, “The WHO Global Logistics Hub in the UAE has become a centerpiece of rapid supply response to acute events globally. Supporting over 140 countries across all 6 WHO geographic regions, the hub provides a lifeline to Member States experiencing health emergencies arising from outbreaks of diseases, disasters such as floods and earthquakes, and the impact of conflict on health systems. The support provided by the UAE enables WHO to be at the forefront of these health emergency responses – delivering specialist medical aid to those in need wherever and whenever it is needed.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Zulu document into English:

"The UAE is primarily focused on providing the most critical and essential supplies in the situation in Sudan, particularly for the sick, children, the elderly, and women who are at risk due to the continuation of the conflict - and these relief consignments will directly address the urgent gaps in healthcare and food provision. The UAE's deep commitment to humanity means it has confirmed its continued engagement with the global community and its dedication to strengthening peace, security, and stability, not only in Sudan but worldwide. Working with partners in the international community, the country continues to assist the Sudanese people during these difficult times. Health supplies, including sufficient medical supplies, essential filtration materials, and vital medicines, have now reached 165,000 of the most vulnerable people in need of humanitarian assistance. Health facilities across the country are reporting that they have run out of basic health supplies and that pharmacies in the country are inaccessible due to the security situation. As further support, the WHO will deploy two emergency logistics hubs to transport the supplies to 13 major health facilities to support health workers and add care for those in need. WHO has 30 large containers of supplies to combat communicable diseases such as measles and cholera, conditions that can be fatal if left untreated. This, along with 23,000 blood bags, has been secured, and the WHO's logistics hub on the ground is located at the International Humanitarian City. WHO is currently exploring all ways to quickly deliver these supplies to Sudan in collaboration with the Sudanese Minister of Health. Dr. Michael Ryan, Executive Director of WHO’s Health Emergencies Programme, said that the WHO Global Logistics Hub in the UAE has become critical for the rapid delivery of supplies in these critical global situations. Supporting more than 140 countries in all regions, the hub provides comprehensive health support to Member States through a robust health emergency response experience, addressing disease outbreaks, disasters such as fires and earthquakes, and the impact of conflict on health systems. The support provided by the UAE has allowed WHO to lead the response to health emergencies – delivering medical assistance to those in need where it is needed most."

translations.source: Njenge ngxenye yokuqinisa ezokwakha umhlaba kwezokulungiselela eziphuthumayo zempilo, ukuphendula nokubekezela Iuae, iWHO nokubambisana bayasebenzisana ukuhambisa amasaplayi ukufinyelela kwababuthaka kakhulu emhlabeni esikhathini sabo abadinga usizo ngayo. Ngengxenye yakhe, uDkt Ahmed Al-Mandhari, uMqondisi wesifunda seWHO wase-Eastern Mediterranean, uthe iNhlangano yeZempilo eMhlabeni izoqhubeka nokuxhumanisa nabasemagunyeni bezempilo eSudan ukuletha amasaplayi kulabo ababuthaka kakhulu emhlabeni esikhathi sokudinga usizo kakhulu. Ngenxa yokuxabana, izibhedlela, amakhemisi nezindawo zokunakekela ezempilo yokuqala seziphelelwe okubalulekile, okuyimithi yokusindisa izimpilo. Ukufika kwamathani amakhulu angeziwe awu-30 okukhathazeka namasaplayi ezokuhlinza okuphuthumayo kanye nemithi ebalulekile ukukwazi iWHO izokweseka izindawo zempilo ezinkulu ezingu-13 nokwenza kabusha kwezempilo umsebenzi wokunakekela kulabo abadingayo. Ilojistikhi yomhhlaba wethu kuhabhu yaseDubai idlala indawo ebaluleke kakhulu ukuqinisekisa ukungaphazamiseki kwesaplayi kwezempilo futhi siyajabula ukwesekwa esikunikiwe yi-United Arab Emirates ukusiza ishatha yokuqala bebhanoyi lalamasaplayi adingeka ngokushesha. Kungukweseka okunjalo njengoba lokhu kukhombisa ukwenza kombono wethu eSifundeni wezempilo kuwona wonke umuntu yiwona wonke umuntu. Usizo lwezokwelapha olunikezwe Inhlangano yezempilo emhlabeni nge-UAE kubalelwe kwafinyelela ku-55% isamba samasaplayi okwelapha anikezwe iSudan asuka phesheya ngo-2022, ukuveza isikhundla seUAE njengehabhu enkulu yosizo loluntu emazweni angaphandle.
translations.reference: As part of efforts to strengthen the global architecture for health emergency preparedness, response, and resilience the UAE, WHO, and partners are working hand-in-hand to deliver supplies to reach the world’s most vulnerable in their time of greatest need.” For his part, Dr Ahmed Al-Mandhari, WHO Regional Director for the Eastern Mediterranean, said, “The World Health Organization continues to coordinate with the Sudanese health authorities to deliver essential health supplies through all available pathways. Due to the conflict, hospitals, pharmacies and primary health care facilities have stocked out of essential, life-saving medicines. The arrival of an additional 30 metric tons of trauma and emergency surgery supplies as well as essential medicines will enable WHO to support 13 major health facilities and re-establish health care services for those in need. Our global logistics hub in Dubai plays a vital role in ensuring an uninterrupted supply of health commodities and we are grateful for the support provided by the United Arab Emirates to facilitate our first charter flight of these desperately needed supplies. It is support such as this that shows in action our Regional vision of health for all, by all.” The medical aid provided by the World Health Organization through the UAE accounted for approximately 55% of the total medical supplies provided to Sudan from abroad in 2022, reflecting the UAE's position as a major hub for international humanitarian assistance.
translations.hypothesis: As part of efforts to strengthen health preparedness for rapid response and resilience, the UAE, WHO and partners are working together to deliver supplies to the most vulnerable people in the world at the time they need help. For its part, Dr. Ahmed Al-Mandhari, Regional Director of the WHO in the Eastern Mediterranean, said that the World Health Organization will continue to connect with health authorities in Sudan to bring supplies to the most vulnerable people in the world at the time of greatest need. Due to the critical situation, hospitals, pharmacies and first aid health facilities are running out of essential items, namely life-saving medicines. The arrival of an additional 30 tons of emergency medical supplies and surgical supplies and essential medicines will enable the WHO to support 13 major health facilities and resume health care work for those in need. Our global logistics hub in Dubai plays a crucial role in ensuring the uninterrupted supply of health supplies and we are pleased with the support we are receiving from the United Arab Emirates to help the first wave of supplies needed urgently. It is support like this that demonstrates the realization of our vision in the Region – health for all, by all. Medical assistance provided by the World Health Organization with the UAE is estimated to have reached 55% of the total medical supplies delivered to Sudan from abroad in 2022, highlighting the UAE's position as a major humanitarian aid hub internationally.

translations.source: Ukubambisana endaweni kubalulekile ekuqhubekeni emhlabeni okuya kokuhlobene nezempilo zamaSDG Umbiko wokuqhubeka omusha ukhishwe ngokuhlanganyela namhlanje kuma ejensi asayinile angu-13 eGlobal Action Plan for Helath Lives and Well being for All (SDG3 GAP). Umbiko usihloko esthi "What worked? What didn’t? What’s next?" uma kubhekwa iminyaka emine kusetshenzwa ndawonye ngama-ejensi ezindlela eziningi ezinkulu kuvezwa ukuthi yikuphi okusebenzile nokungasebenzanga ekuqinisweni ukubambisana nokwesekwa kwenqubeko yezwe okuuya nokuhlobene nezempilo zeSustainable Development Goals (amaSDG). Lo nyaka umaka iphuzu lahhafu eliya kumaSDG kodwa umhlaba usele ngemuva ukufika kuzinhloso zomhlaba. Kusukela ngokwethulwa kwayo ngo-2019, iSDG3 GAP yenze ukubambisana okusha kwezinhlaka phakathi kokusayina kwama ejensi ezindaweni ezibalulekile njengokugcineka kwezimali nokunakekelwa kwezempilo kokuqala kwabanye abantu. Okungenani amazwe angu-67 akhulumile kanye noma ngaphezulu ngeSDG3 GAP ukuqhuba izingqikithi. Senze inqubeko ebalulekile, kodwa kusanendlela ende okumele siyihambe ukwenza ngcono indlela izinhlangano eziningi ezisebenzisana ngayo ukweseka amazwe. Kumele silalele esikutshelwa ngamazwe futhi silandele imiyalelo,kusho uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, uMqondisi Jikelele weWHO noSihlalo weSDG3 GAP kuzinhloko zeqoqo. "Ngibonga ababambisani ngokubambisana kwazo nokuzihlola ngokwethembeka okuqukethwe kulombiko. "Umbiko unikeza iziphakamiso eziyisithupha ezibalulekile ukuqinisekisa ukuthi amazwe ayazuza ekwesekweni okukhishiwe nokubona ukuzibophezela okwenziwe kuSDG3 GAP.
translations.reference: Collaborating locally is key for progress globally towards health-related SDGs A new progress report is being jointly released today by the 13 signatory agencies of the Global Action Plan for Healthy Lives and Well-being for All (SDG3 GAP). The report entitled “What worked? What didn’t? What’s next?” reflects four years of joint work by major multilateral agencies outlining what has and has not worked in strengthening collaboration and support to accelerate country progress towards health-related Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). This year marks the halfway point toward the SDGs, but the world is lagging behind to reach the global goals. Since its launch in 2019, the SDG3 GAP enabled creation of new collaboration structures between the signatory agencies in key areas such as sustainable financing and primary health care among others. At least 67 countries engaged in on one or more of the SDG3 GAP accelerator themes. "We have made important progress, but we still have a long path to travel to improve the way that multilateral organizations work together to support countries. We must listen to what countries tell us and act upon their guidance,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General and Chair of the SDG3 GAP Principals Group. “I thank the partners for their collaboration and for the honest self-assessments contained in this report. "The report provides six key recommendations to ensure that countries benefit from a more streamlined support and to realize the commitments made in the SDG3 GAP.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Zulu document into English:

"Collaboration at the local level is crucial for the continuation of global efforts related to health and the SDGs. A new progress report was released today jointly by 13 signatory agencies of the Global Action Plan for Health Lives and Well-being for All (SDG3 GAP). The report is titled "What worked? What didn’t? What’s next?" Examining four years of working together by agencies across various large-scale approaches reveals what has worked and what hasn't in strengthening collaboration and supporting national progress related to the health targets of the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). This year marks the halfway point to the SDGs, but the world is lagging behind in achieving global goals. Since its launch in 2019, the SDG3 GAP has fostered new collaboration between agencies in critical areas such as financial sustainability and primary health care for vulnerable populations. At least 67 countries have engaged with the SDG3 GAP to drive initiatives. We have made significant progress, but there is still a long way to go to improve how many organizations work together to support countries. We must listen to what countries tell us and follow their guidance," said Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of the WHO and Chair of the SDG3 GAP, in the report's highlights. "I thank the partners for their collaboration and their dedicated self-reflection, which is contained in this report." The report provides six key recommendations to ensure that countries benefit from the support provided and see the commitment made to the SDG3 GAP.

translations.source: Lokhu kufaka: Ukuqhubeka kokwenza ngcono kweSDG3 GAP kwezempilo ngokufuna imibono yeMember State ukuthi sibambisana kanjani ezingeni lezwe nokuphendula okuhlobene neziphakamiso; Ukugcina iGAP njengenkundla yokubambisana; ukulondoloza ukubambisana okuqinile ezingeni lezwe ekunakekelweni kwezempilo kokuqala nokubheka izindlela ezintsha njengesimo sezulu sokubekezela kuzinhlelo zempilo; Ngokuhlanganyela sebenzisa izindlela ezintsha ezingeni lezwe njengokuhambisa indlela yomthelela; ukukhuluma kakhulu nomphakathi; kanye nokusebenza neMember States ukuqinisa imiklomelo yokubambisana ngobuholi bezepolitiki,indlela yokubusa noxhasomali lokweseka ukubambisana. Imiphumela ebalulekile yombiko sizoyifaka ezingxoxweni kuUnited Nations General Assembly SDG Summit elandelayo nomhlangano wezinga eliphezulu kuUniversal Helath Coverage ngoMandulo 2023. Izinyanga eziholela kulezigameko ezibalulekile zinikeza ithuba ukuphusha ndawonye yiMember States kamaejensi ezindlela eziningi ukuhlonza izindlela ukuthi kuzokwenziwa kanjani iziphakamiso ukulungiselella indawo yesibambiso esiphelele ukuqina kokubambisana nokuqhuba inqubeko eya kokuhlobene nezempilo SDG kuhhafu wesibili weSDG wesikhathi esibekiwe. Inothi eliya kumhleli:
translations.reference: These include: continue the SDG3 GAP improvement cycle for health by seeking the views of Member States on how we collaborate at country-level and responding to related recommendations; maintain GAP as a platform for collaboration; foster stronger collaboration at the country level on primary health care and explore new thematic topics such as climate resilient health systems; jointly apply new approaches at country level such as the delivery for impact approach; engage more with civil society; and work with Member States to strengthen incentives for collaboration through political leadership, governance direction and funding to support collaboration. Key findings of the report will feed into discussions of the next United Nations General Assembly SDG Summit and the High-level Meeting on Universal Health Coverage in September 2023. The months leading up to these key events provide an opportunity for a joint push by Member States and multilateral agencies to identify ways on how to implement the recommendations and to prepare the ground to fully leverage stronger collaboration and accelerate progress towards the health-related SDGs in the second half of the SDG timeframe. Note to the editor:
translations.hypothesis:

**Translation:**

This includes: Continuing the improvement of the SDG3 GAP in health by seeking Member State input on how we can collaborate at the national level and respond to proposals; Maintaining the GAP as a platform for collaboration; preserving strong collaboration at the national level in primary healthcare and exploring new approaches such as climate resilience for health systems; Jointly utilizing new approaches at the national level such as delivering the pathway to impact; greater community engagement; and working with Member States to strengthen incentives for collaboration through political leadership, governance, and support to foster collaboration. Key findings of the report will be included in discussions at the next United Nations General Assembly SDG Summit and the high-level meeting on Universal Health Coverage in July 2023. The months leading up to these important events offer an opportunity for Member States, as agents of multiple approaches, to identify ways to implement proposals to prepare the space for full commitment, sustainability of collaboration, and driving progress towards health SDG targets in the second half of the SDG timeframe. Note to the editor:

The signatories to the SDG3 GAP are Gavi, the Vaccine Alliance; Global Financing Facility for Women, Children and Adolescents (GFF); Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria (Global Fund); International Labour Organization (ILO); Joint United Nations Programme on HIV/AIDS (UNAIDS); United Nations Development Programme (UNDP); United Nations Population Fund (UNFPA); United Nations Children’s Fund (UNICEF); Unitaid; United Nations Entity for Gender Equality and the Empowerment of Women (UN Women);

translations.reference: World Bank Group; World Food Programme (WFP); and World Health Organization (WHO). See full report, What Worked? What’s next?
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Zulu text:

"The World Bank collection; The World Food Programme (WFP); and the World Health Organization (WHO). See the full report, What works? What’s next?"

**Explanation of Choices:**

*   **Iqoqo lebhange lomhlaba:** Translated as "The World Bank collection" - This accurately reflects the meaning of the phrase.
*   **Uhlelo lwezokudla emhlabeni (WFP):** Translated as "The World Food Programme (WFP)" -  Keeping the acronym (WFP) is standard practice.
*   **NeNhlangano Yezempilo Emhlabeni (WHO):** Translated as "the World Health Organization (WHO)" - Again, keeping the acronym (WHO) is standard.
*   **Bona umbiko ogcwele:** Translated as "See the full report" - A direct and clear translation.
*   **Yikuphi okusebenzile? Yikuphi okulandelayo?:** Translated as "What works? What’s next?" - This captures the essence of the questions being posed.

translations.source: Imiphumela yocwaningo olusha ikhombisa izinhlelo zempilo ziqala ukuba ngcono esikhathini sobhubhane Ngemva kweminyaka emithathu yobhubhane lweCOVID-19 izinhlelo zempilo emazweni seziqale ukukhombisa izinkomba ezinkulu zokuvuseleleka kwezinhlelo zempilo, ngokweWHO umbiko wesikhashana emzuliswaneni wesine wocwaningo lephalisi yomhlaba ekuqhubekeni kwemisebenzi yezempilo ebalulekile ngesikhathi sobhubhane lweCOVID-19: Lwezi 2022-Masingana 2023. Ngokuqala kuka-2023 amazwe abike ukwenzeka kokwehla kokuphazamiseka ekulethweni kwendlela yemisebenzi yezempilo,kodwa kwavezwa isidingo sokutshala kokungalungiswa nokubekezela kakhulu kwikusasa. Kulamazwe angu-139 aphendule kumzuliswane wesine wokuzwakala kocwaningo lweWHO, amazwe abike ukuqhubeka kokuphazamiseka emisebenzini yekota esilinganisweni. Emazweni angu-84 lapho ukuhlaziya okudumile kungenzeka,iphesenti lemisebenzi ephazamisekile yehlile ngesilinganiso esisuka ku-56% ngoNtulikazi kuya kuMandulo 2020 ngo-23 % ngoLwezi 2022-Masingana 2023. Ukuqhubeka kokuphazamiseka kungenxa yakho kokubili ukufuneka nokusaplaya izinkinga eziseceleni, okufaka amazinga aphansi okunakekelwa kwezempilo emphakathini okudingayo kanye nomkhawulo wokubakhona kwabasebenzi bezempilo nezinsizakusebenza ezinye zokunakekelwa kwezempilo njengokuvulwa kwemitholampilo noma ukubakhona kwesitoko semithi nemikhiqizo. "Izindaba ezamukelwayo ukuthi izinhlelozempilo emzaweni amaningi ziqala ukuvuselela imisebenzi yezempilo ebalulekile ezigidini zabantu abawugejile ngesikhathi sobhubhane," kusho uDkt Rudi Eggers, Umqondisi weWHO weMisebenzi yeZempilo yoKuhlanganisiwe. "Kodwa sidinga ukuqinisekisa ukuthi wonke amazwe ayaqhubeka nokuvala izikhala ukuvuselela imisebenzi yezempilo,nokusebenzisa izifundo abazifundile ukwakha izinhlelo zempilo ezizilungiselele nokubekezela kwekusasa". Izimpawu zokuqala eziqaphelekayo ekuvuseleleni Kulolu cwaningo olusha,amazwe ambalwa abike ngokungenhloso isikalo sasemuva sokuthola kuzinkundla zonke umsebenzi wokuletha nokusebenza kwezempilo yomphakathi okubalulekile kusukela 2020-2021 ukubikwa kukhombisa ukubaluleka kwegxathu kubuyelwe emazingeni angaphambi kobhubhane ukulethwa kokusebenza nokusebenza okukhulu kwezinhlelo.
translations.reference: New survey results show health systems starting to recover from pandemic After three years of the COVID-19 pandemic, health systems in countries have started showing the first major signs of health system recovery, according to the WHO interim report on the “Fourth round of the global pulse survey on continuity of essential health services during the COVID-19 pandemic: November 2022–January 2023”. By early 2023, countries reported experiencing reduced disruptions in the delivery of routine health services, but highlighted the need to invest in recovery and stronger resilience for the future. Among the 139 countries that responded to the fourth round of WHO’s pulse survey, countries reported continued disruptions in almost one-quarter of services on average. In 84 countries where trend analysis is possible, the percentage of disrupted services declined on average from 56% in July-September 2020 to 23% in November 2022- January 2023. Persisting disruptions are due to both demand- and supply-side factors, including low levels of health care-seeking in communities as well as limited availability of health workers and other health-care resources such as open clinics or available stocks of medicines and products. “It is welcome news that health systems in the majority of countries are starting to restore essential health services for millions of people who missed them during the pandemic,” said Dr Rudi Eggers, WHO Director for Integrated Health Services. “But we need to ensure that all countries continue to close this gap to recover health services, and apply lessons learnt to build more prepared and resilient health systems for the future”. First notable signs of recovery In this new survey, fewer countries reported intentionally scaling back access across all service delivery platforms and essential public health functions since 2020-2021 reporting, showing an important step to return to pre-pandemic levels of service delivery and broader system functioning.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Zulu document into English:

"The findings of recent research indicate that health systems are beginning to improve during the pandemic. After three years of the COVID-19 pandemic, health systems in countries are starting to show significant signs of recovery, according to a WHO interim report in the fourth round of research on the pulse survey on the continuity of essential health services during the COVID-19 pandemic: 2022-September 2023. At the beginning of 2023, countries reported the possibility of a decrease in disruptions to the delivery of health services, but emphasized the need for continued investment and resilience for the future. In the 139 countries that responded to the WHO research pulse survey’s fourth round, countries reported a continuation of disruptions to integrated health services. In 84 countries where comprehensive analysis was possible, the percentage of disrupted services decreased from 56% in July 2020 to 23% in September 2022-2023. The continued disruption is due to both the need to address and manage secondary challenges, including low levels of community health care, and the limited availability of health workers and resources for some health services, such as the opening of hospitals or the availability of medicines and products. "The encouraging news is that health systems in many countries are beginning to restore essential health services for populations that were left behind during the pandemic," said Dr. Rudi Eggers, WHO Director of Integrated Health Operations. "But we need to ensure that all countries continue to close gaps in restoring health services and use the lessons they have learned to build health systems that are resilient and prepared for the future." Early signs of recovery In this new research, a few countries have inadvertently reported a recent event of recovering public health service delivery and performance from 2020-2021, with reporting showing a return to pre-pandemic levels of delivery and increased system performance."

translations.source: Ekupheleni kuka-2022, amazwe amaningi abike izimpawu ezingacacile zokuvuselelwa kokusebenza okufaka ukusebenza kwezocansi, ukuzala, abakhullwe, abazelwe, izingane nezempilo yentsha, ukudla okunempilo ukugomma,izifo ezithelelanayo (okufaka umalaleveva, iHIV, iTB nezinye izifo ezithelelana ngokocansi) izifo ezidumile ezinganakiwe,izifo okungaxhunyanwa ngazo,ukuphathwa kwengqondo, okwengqondo nesifo sokusebenzisa izidakamiza, ukunakekelwa kwabantu abadala nokunakekelwa okulekelelayo nokwesiko. Inombolo yamazwe abika ukuphazamiseka ohlelweni lwawo lesaplayi sheni ezweni okungafika kuhhafu (29 ka-59 wamazwe aphendulayo) okunganekota (18 wa-66 wamazwe) aphedulayo kulonyaka odlule. Ngaphandle kwezinkomba zokuvuselela,ukuphazamiseka komsebenzi kuyaqhubeka emazweni onke namazinga emali engenayo kuzona zonke izinhlelo soomsebenzi wokulatha nokulandelela umsebenzi ezindaweni. Amazwe aphinde abhekane nokunyuka komsebenzi okumele wenziwe - ikakhulukazi emisebenzini yokubheka, ukuxilonga nokwelapha izifo okungaxhunyanwa ngazo - okungaholela kumiphumela engemihle uma abantu bebambezeleka ukuthola ukunakekelwa ngesikhathi. Ukuvuselelwa kwemisebenzi yezempilo ebalulekile kubucayi ngenxa yokuphazamiseka - okufaka imisebenzi yokugqugquzela ezempilo, ukuvinjwa kwezifo, ukwelapha, ukuvuselelwa kwesimilo nephaliyeshini - kungaba nokukhulu umthelela ohlukene wezempil nezinga lomuntu ukunobhubhane qobo ikakhulukazi kubantu babuthaka. Ukuhlanganisa imisebenzi yeCOVID-19 emisebenzini yezempilo ebalulekile elinye igxathu elibalulekile eliya ekuvuselelweni kohlelo noguquko,amazwe amaningi asebe nenqubekela phambili ekuhlanganiseni imisebenzi yeCOVID-19 endleleni yokuletha imisebenzi yezempilo. Cishe u-80%-90% wamazwe ahlanganyele ngokuphelele ekugonyweni kweCOVID-19 ukuxilonga nemisebenzi yokuphathwa kwecala kanye nemisebenzi yesimo sangemuva kweCOVID-19 endleleni yomsebenzi wokuletha. Kuyafana amazwe amaningi (u-80% wamazwe awu-83 aphendulile) abike okungenani okuncane ekukhuphukeni kokuthola amathuluzi abalulekile eCovid-19 (isibonelo ukuxilongwa kweCOVID-19, ukwelaphangxube, imigomo namathuluzi okuzivikela siqu - PPE), ngezinkinga zokusebenza kwezempilo nokwentuleka koxhasomali okumele izivimbo ezijwayeleke kakhulu. Ukuqhubeka kokusekwa kuyadingeka ukuvuselela,ukubekezela nokuzilungiselela Amazwe amaningi aseqalile asebenzisa asebekufundile ngesikhathi sobhubhane lweCOVID-19 okufaka ngezikhungo zenamba yokusbenza kokusha ukuphazamisa amasu endleleni yokulethwa kwezempilo.
translations.reference: By the end of 2022, most countries reported partial signs of service recovery, including in services for sexual, reproductive, maternal, newborn, child and adolescent health; nutrition; immunization; communicable diseases (including malaria, HIV, TB, and other sexually-transmitted infections); neglected tropical diseases; noncommunicable diseases; management of mental, neurological and substance use disorders; care for older people; and traditional and/or complementary care. The number of countries reporting disruption to their national supply chain system reduced from nearly half (29 of 59 responding countries) to about a quarter (18 of 66 responding countries) within the last year. Despite signs of recovery, service disruptions persist across countries in all regions and income levels, and across most service delivery settings and tracer service areas. Countries are also dealing with increasing service backlogs – most frequently in services for screening, diagnosis and treatment of noncommunicable diseases – which can lead to negative consequences as people are delayed access to timely care. Recovering essential health service delivery is critical because disruptions – including to services for health promotion, disease prevention, diagnosis, treatment, rehabilitation and palliation – may have even greater adverse health effects at population and individual level than the pandemic itself, especially among vulnerable populations. Integrating COVID-19 services into essential health services In another important step towards system recovery and transition, most countries have made progress in integrating COVID-19 services into routine health service delivery. About 80-90% of countries have fully integrated COVID-19 vaccination, diagnostic and case management services as well as services for post COVID-19 condition into routine service delivery. Still, most countries (80% of 83 responding countries) reported at least one bottleneck to scaling up access to essential COVID-19 tools (e.g. COVID-19 diagnostics, therapeutics, vaccines and personal protective equipment - PPE), with health workforce issues and lack of funding representing the most common barriers. Further support needed for recovery, resilience and preparedness Most countries have started to apply what they have learnt during the COVID-19 pandemic, including through the institutionalization of a number of innovative service disruption mitigation strategies into routine health service delivery.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Zulu document into English:

"Towards the end of 2022, many countries reported signs of a recovery in essential health services, including sexual and reproductive health, maternal health, newborn and child health, nutrition, immunization, communicable diseases (including measles, HIV, TB, and other sexually transmitted infections), neglected tropical diseases, non-communicable diseases, mental health, mental disorders and substance use, care of older adults, and palliative and hospice care. The number of countries reporting disruptions to their health service delivery was significant, reaching almost half (29 of 59 responding countries) for immunization and a little over a quarter (18 of 66 responding countries) last year. Beyond the signs of recovery, service delivery disruptions continue across countries and income levels in all health programs for data collection and workforce tracking. Countries are also facing an increase in the workload required – particularly in the diagnosis, screening, and treatment of non-communicable diseases – which can lead to negative outcomes if people experience delays in accessing care. The recovery of essential health services is critical given the disruptions – including services for health promotion, disease prevention, treatment, rehabilitation, and palliative care – and can have a diverse impact on health and mortality, particularly for vulnerable populations. Integrating COVID-19 activities into essential health services is another important step towards system recovery and transformation; many countries have made progress in integrating COVID-19 activities into the health service delivery pathway. Approximately 80%-90% of countries have fully integrated COVID-19 case identification and management activities, as well as post-COVID-19 condition management, into the service delivery pathway. Similarly, most countries (80% of 83 respondents) reported at least some challenges in accessing essential COVID-19 tools (e.g., COVID-19 testing, therapeutics, vaccines, and personal protective equipment - PPE), due to health service disruptions and supply chain shortages that are most commonly reported. Continued support is needed to accelerate recovery, resilience, and preparedness. Many countries have started to use what they have learned during the COVID-19 pandemic, including innovative work models and strategies to mitigate disruptions to health service delivery."

translations.source: Lokhu kufaka ukubekwa kwezindlela zokwelapha ngefoni,ukugqugquzela ukunakekelwa usekhaya nokungenelela kokuzinakekela, izindlela zokuqinisa ukubakhona kwabasebenzi bezempilo, amandla nezindlela zokweseka, ukwenza kabusha ekuthengeni nokuletha imithi namasaplayi ezinye izindlela zokuxhumana nomphakathi ukubambisana nabanikezeli bomkhakha ozimele nowomphakathi. Amakota amathathu ezwe abaike ukufakwa koxhasomali olwengeziwe oluya ekuvuselelweni kohlelo lwesikhathi eside, ukubekezela nokuzilungiselela. Amazwe agcizelele isidingo sokwesekwa kweWHO ekubhekaneni nezinkinga ezisele ngokwengqikithi yeCOVID-19 nangale,ikakhulukazi okujwayele ukuhlobana nokuqinisa ukusebenza kwezempilo, ukwakha amandla okubheka imisebenzi yezempilo, ukwakha ukunakekelwa kwezempilo kokuqala okuncike kuzindlela zokunakekela, ukubusa, inqubomgomo nokuhlela nokuhlela ezezimali noxhasomali. Inothi kubahleli: Emzuliswaneni wesine wocwaningo lwephasile yomhlaba weWHO, amazwe angu-222, izindawo bamenywa ukuba baphendule kucwaningo oluncike kuwebhu eqinisikesiwe phakathi kukaLwezi 2022 noMasingana 2023. Ucwaningo lwesine lulandele kucwaningo lwephasile edlule yango-2020 no2021 lweWHO: uMzuliswano 1 (Nhlaba-Mandulo 2020) uMzuliswano 2 (Masingana-Ndasa 2021) noMzuliswano 3 (Lwezi-Zibandlela 2021) okwakhombisa ukukhulu bokuthi ubhubhane lalukhahlameza ukuqhubeka kwemisebenzi yezempilo ebalulekile nokuthi amazwe enza kanjani. Ngesikhathi ucwaningo lwephasile lunemikhawulo njengokubika ukuchema nokumela,amandla alomzamo ukuthi mukhulu futhi uletha ulwazi ngokuphindekile. Itemu "izwe" kumele liqondwe ukuthi lifaka wonke amazwe, imingcele nezindawo. Abahlaziya okudumile kwaqedwa kuncike ezimpendulweni eziphuma emazweni angu-84, abahlukumezi noma izindawo ezaphendula okungenani kusahluko socwaningo olulodwa kuyona yonke imizuliswano emine yocwaningo.
translations.reference: These include deployment of telemedicine approaches, promotion of home-based care or self-care interventions, approaches for strengthening health workers availability, capacities and support mechanisms, innovations in procuring and delivering medicines and supplies, more routine community communications, and partnerships with private sector providers. Three quarters of countries reported additional funding allocation towards longer term system recovery, resilience and preparedness. Countries expressed need for WHO support to address remaining challenges in the COVID-19 context and beyond, most frequently related to health workforce strengthening, building the monitoring capacities of health services, designing primary health care -- oriented models of care, governance, policy and planning and financial planning and funding. Note to editors: In the fourth round of WHO’s global pulse survey, 222 countries, territories and areas were invited to respond to a standardized web-based survey between November 2022 and January 2023. The fourth survey followed up on WHO’s previous 2020 and 2021 pulse surveys: Round 1 (May-September 2020), Round 2 (January-March 2021), and Round 3 (November-December 2021) which showed the extent to which the pandemic was affecting the continuity of essential health services and how countries are taking action. While pulse surveys have limitations such as reporting bias and representativeness, the strength of this effort is that it is comprehensive and delivers information rapidly. The term “country” should be understood to include all countries, territories and areas. Trend analyses was completed based on responses from 84 countries, territories, or areas that responded to at least one survey section in all four pulse survey rounds.
